en.extract:
The spotted wren (Campylorhynchus gularis) is a species of bird in the family Troglodytidae. It is endemic to Mexico.


Taxonomy and systematics
The spotted wren has sometimes been considered conspecific with Boucard's wren (Campylorhynchus jocosus) but molecular data show that they are not closely related. The species is monotypic.


Description
The spotted wren is 17 cm (6.7 in) long and weighs 28.3 to 31 g (1.00 to 1.09 oz). Adults of both sexes have a chestnut crown and rufescent brown shoulders and back with dull black and buffy markings. The tail is gray-brown with darker bars. They have a white supercilium and a brown eyestripe above buffy white cheeks. Their chin, throat, and chest are off-white and the sides of the chest have blackish spots. The belly and lower flanks are buffy with faint barring. The juvenile's crown is dull black, the markings on the back are fainter than the adults', and the chest is pale buff without the adults' spots.


Distribution and habitat
The spotted wren is found in two areas of Mexico separated by unsuitable habitat. In the west it ranges from southeastern Sonora south to western México state and western Morelos. East of that band it ranges from southern Nuevo León, southwestern Tamaulipas, and central San Luis Potosí south to Hidalgo. It occurs in several habitat types, from dry oak and pine-oak woodlands to stands of shrubs and cactus to rocky slopes with palms. In elevation it is generally found between 800 and 2,500 m (2,600 and 8,200 ft) but ranges up to 3,000 m (9,800 ft) in Colima.


Behavior


Feeding
The spotted wren actively forages in small groups, usually on or near the ground but as high as 15 m (49 ft). It probes bark crevices, epiphytes, and among rocks for invertebrates. It also eats vegetable matter like cactus seeds and has been seen to prey on small lizards.


Breeding
The spotted wren's breeding phenology is virtually unknown. It builds a domed nest with  side entrance like others of its genus. The clutch size appears to be two to four.


Vocalization
The spotted wren's song has been described as "a series of harsh churring notes" and "a series of unmusical gurgling phrases" [1] [2]. Its call is "a gruff 'cheh-cheh-cheht'" [3].


Status
The IUCN has assessed the spotted wren as being of Least Concern. "Abundance varies; quite sparse and uncommon in some areas, and very common in suitable habitat in others."


References

The '''spotted wren''' ('''''Campylorhynchus gularis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Troglodytidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Mexico]].<ref name=IOC11.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |title=IOC World Bird List (v 11.1) |last1=Gill |first1= F. |last2=Donsker|first2=D.|last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. |date=January 2021 |access-date=January 14, 2021 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The spotted wren has sometimes been considered [[conspecific]] with Boucard's wren (''Campylorhynchus jocosus'') but molecular data show that they are not closely related. The species is [[monotypic]].<ref name=SPWR-BOW>Kroodsma, D. E., D. Brewer, and E. de Juana (2020). Spotted Wren (''Campylorhynchus gularis''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.spowre1.01 retrieved May 31, 2021</ref>

==Description==

The spotted wren is {{convert|17|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|28.3|to|31|g|oz|abbr=on}}. Adults of both sexes have a chestnut crown and rufescent brown shoulders and back with dull black and buffy markings. The tail is gray-brown with darker bars. They have a white [[supercilium]] and a brown eyestripe above buffy white cheeks. Their chin, throat, and chest are off-white and the sides of the chest have blackish spots. The belly and lower flanks are buffy with faint barring. The juvenile's crown is dull black, the markings on the back are fainter than the adults', and the chest is pale buff without the adults' spots.<ref name=SPWR-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==

The spotted wren is found in two areas of Mexico separated by unsuitable habitat. In the west it ranges from southeastern [[Sonora]] south to western [[State of Mexico|México]] state and western [[Morelos]]. East of that band it ranges from southern [[Nuevo León]], southwestern [[Tamaulipas]], and central [[San Luis Potosí]] south to [[Hidalgo (state)|Hidalgo]]. It occurs in several habitat types, from dry oak and pine-oak woodlands to stands of shrubs and cactus to rocky slopes with palms. In elevation it is generally found between {{convert|800|and|2500|m|ft|abbr=on}} but ranges up to {{convert|3000|m|ft|abbr=on}} in [[Colima]].<ref name=SPWR-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Feeding===

The spotted wren actively forages in small groups, usually on or near the ground but as high as {{convert|15|m|ft|abbr=on}}. It probes bark crevices, [[epiphyte]]s, and among rocks for invertebrates. It also eats vegetable matter like cactus seeds and has been seen to prey on small lizards.<ref name=SPWR-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The spotted wren's breeding [[phenology]] is virtually unknown. It builds a domed nest with  side entrance like others of its genus. The clutch size appears to be two to four.<ref name=SPWR-BOW/>

===Vocalization===

The spotted wren's song has been described as "a series of harsh churring notes" and "a series of unmusical gurgling phrases" [https://www.xeno-canto.org/451685] [https://www.xeno-canto.org/351627]. Its call is "a gruff 'cheh-cheh-cheht'" [https://www.xeno-canto.org/451696].<ref name=SPWR-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the spotted wren as being of Least Concern.<ref name=IUCN/> "Abundance varies; quite sparse and uncommon in some areas, and very common in suitable habitat in others."<ref name=SPWR-BOW/>
